turns.content: I've read about macros in Rust but I still don't get how they actually work. I thought maybe it was just a fancy way to write regular code without using the macro syntax at all?

That's a common misunderstanding! Macros aren't preprocessor directives like in C or C++; they are expanded by the compiler before compilation happens. So when you use `macro_rules!` to define something, it gets fully replaced with actual code during compilation. You can combine this with crates like rstest to generate many test cases quickly without writing them out manually.
Oh okay so I don't have to write all those test functions myself? Can you give me an example of how that looks in code?

Sure! You define a macro using `macro_rules!` and then call it inside your test function. For instance, if you want to run tests for different values like 1 through 5, you can write just one line with the macro that expands into multiple calls to the actual test functions.
Wait so it actually generates the code? Like does Rust create new files or something?

No, nothing gets written to disk. It happens entirely in memory during compilation. The macro `macro_rules!` takes input tokens and produces output tokens that are then compiled into real functions by the compiler itself.
